plainMeaning: On Mount Aṣṭāpada — where the first Jina Ṛṣabha gave his sermon and then passed into liberation — I sing the praise of Ṛṣabha.
recitation: usabhassa samosaraṇā, payapaṅkaya aṅkaṇāu sivagamaṇā | tava laddhi rohaga siddhi, uya aṭṭhāvayaṁmi thuṇe usabhaṁ ||

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit genitive sg. of usabha < Sanskrit ṛṣabha ('bull', an epithet of the first Jina); -ssa is the Prakrit gen. ending.
wordByWord.notes: Names Ādinātha / Ṛṣabhadeva, whose tīrtha this verse celebrates.

wordByWord.gloss: the samavasaraṇa (divine preaching-assembly) — here, the site of it
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit samosaraṇa < Sanskrit samavasaraṇa (sam-ava-√sṛ, 'to come together'); the assembly-hall a Jina occupies after kevala-jñāna.
wordByWord.notes: Read in apposition with Aṣṭāpada: the mountain that held Ṛṣabha's samavasaraṇa.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit paya (< Sanskrit pāda, 'foot') + paṅkaya (< paṅkaja, 'born of mud' = lotus); a stock honorific for a holy being's feet.

wordByWord.gloss: by/through the imprinting (the stamping of his footprints)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ablative/instrumental of aṅkaṇa (< Sanskrit aṅkana, √aṅk 'to mark, stamp'); -āu is the Prakrit abl. ending.
wordByWord.notes: Aṣṭāpada is hallowed as bearing the mark of Ṛṣabha's lotus-feet.

wordByWord.gloss: departure to śiva (going to the place of liberation)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit siva (< Sanskrit śiva, 'the auspicious / liberation') + gamaṇa (< gamana, √gam 'to go'); 'the going-to-liberation', i.e. nirvāṇa.
wordByWord.notes: Refers to Ṛṣabha's mokṣa attained on this very mountain.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit tava < Sanskrit tapas (√tap 'to heat, to be austere'); the heat of penance that ripens spiritual power.

wordByWord.gloss: labdhi — supernormal attainment/power won through tapas
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit laddhi < Sanskrit labdhi (√labh 'to obtain'); the special faculties (e.g. jaṅghā-cāraṇa) gained by austerity.
wordByWord.notes: tava-laddhi = 'the labdhi born of austerity', construed with the climbing of Aṣṭāpada.

wordByWord.gloss: ascending, climbing (the act of going up)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit rohaga < Sanskrit ā-roha / rohaka (√ruh 'to ascend, mount'); 'a climber' or 'climbing'.
wordByWord.notes: Alludes to the tradition that Aṣṭāpada can be scaled only by one possessing labdhi.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit/Sanskrit siddhi (√sidh 'to succeed, be accomplished'); 'achievement, perfection'.
wordByWord.notes: tava-laddhi-rohaga-siddhi reads as a chain: 'the accomplishment of ascending by the power of austerity'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit particle, commonly < Sanskrit uta ('and, also, indeed') or an interjection 'lo, behold'.
wordByWord.notes: Sense is mildly uncertain; taken as a connective leading into the main clause.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit locative of aṭṭhāvaya < Sanskrit aṣṭāpada ('eight-footed/eight-stepped'); -ṁmi is the Prakrit loc. ending.
wordByWord.notes: The pilgrimage place this verse-cluster (vv.41–45) celebrates.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit 1st-person sg. of thuṇai < Sanskrit √stu (stunoti, 'to praise, laud').
wordByWord.notes: The hymn's recurring gesture of homage, here applied to Ṛṣabha.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit accusative sg. of usabha < Sanskrit ṛṣabha; -ṁ is the Prakrit acc. ending.
wordByWord.notes: Echoes usabhassa in pāda 1, framing the verse with the Jina's name at start and close.

literalTranslation: Of Ṛṣabha [it held] the samavasaraṇa; by the imprint of [his] lotus-feet [it is marked]; [it is the place of his] departure to liberation; and [where] the accomplishment of climbing [comes] through the power born of austerity — on Aṣṭāpada I praise Ṛṣabha.
idiomaticTranslation: On Aṣṭāpada — the mountain that once held Ṛṣabha's preaching-assembly, that bears the print of his lotus-feet, from which he passed into liberation, and whose heights yield only to one whose austerities have ripened into spiritual power — there I lift my praise to Ṛṣabha.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse is the devotional climax of the Aṣṭāpada cluster (vv.41–45), gathering four glories of the mountain into a single breath before the closing act of praise. First, samosaraṇā: Aṣṭāpada held Ṛṣabha's preaching-assembly, so it is sanctified by the Jina's living teaching presence. Second, payapaṅkaya-aṅkaṇāu: the very rock is 'stamped' by his lotus-feet — the place is a relic of his bodily passage. Third, sivagamaṇā: it is his nirvāṇa-bhūmi, the ground of his final liberation, which (per the tradition recalled in the preface's structure-arc) makes it one of the supreme crossing-places. Fourth, and most distinctive, tava-laddhi-rohaga-siddhi: success in ascending Aṣṭāpada is won only through labdhi — supernormal faculty ripened by austerity. This compressed phrase encodes the well-known conviction that the summit cannot be reached by ordinary footing; one needs the ascetic's power (the tradition that Gautamasvāmī ascended by jaṅghā-cāraṇa-labdhi stands behind it). The poet thus turns a geographic fact — a peak hard to climb — into a doctrinal lesson: the holy heights are gained by inner discipline, not muscle. The verse is ring-framed: usabhassa opens it, usabhaṁ (object of thuṇe, 'I praise') seals it, so the whole utterance is bracketed by the name of the Jina it honours. Note how each clause names a different kind of trace the Jina leaves on a place — his speech, his footprint, his liberation, and the spiritual prerequisite for approaching him — building a layered theology of sacred site before resolving into the simple, total act: 'I praise Ṛṣabha.'
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on two Jain principles. (1) Nirvāṇa-bhūmi sanctity: the ground where a Tīrthaṅkara attains mokṣa becomes a perpetual tīrtha, worthy of veneration even after the liberated soul has departed — devotion is to the path the Jina walked, not to a present deity. (2) Labdhi as the fruit of tapas: austerity (tapas, one of the means of nirjarā) ripens into supernormal attainments; here that doctrine is dramatised as the literal condition for ascending the mountain, quietly teaching that proximity to the liberated is achieved by self-discipline.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, the worshipper performs a mental pilgrimage to Aṣṭāpada and is reminded that the 'climb' to the Jina is interior: the heights open not to status or strength but to ripened austerity and discipline. It invites a renewed resolve toward tapas and restraint as the real means of drawing near to liberation.

crossReferences.text: Ṛṣabha's nirvāṇa on Aṣṭāpada with ten thousand monks; Bharata's shrine of the eight steps
Gautamasvāmī's ascent of Aṣṭāpada by jaṅghā-cāraṇa-labdhi and the 'Jagacintāmaṇi' caityavandana to its images

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit-English Dictionary (1899) — Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon

sources.title: Paia-Sadda-Mahannavo (full scanned text), H. D. T. Sheth — Internet Archive
sources.consultedFor: Cross-checking Prakrit case-endings (-ssa gen., -āu abl., -ṁmi loc.) and verbal forms

notes: Pāda 2 (tava-laddhi-rohaga-siddhi) is a compressed Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa string parsed here as 'success in ascending [Aṣṭāpada] through the labdhi born of austerity', echoing the tradition that the mountain yields only to one with ascetic power; alternative parsings reading it as a generic list of attainments (tapas, labdhi, ascent, siddhi) won at the site are possible. The particle 'uya' (ઉય) is taken as a connective/emphatic (≈ uta, 'and so / indeed'); its precise force is mildly uncertain. 'siva' is read as śiva = liberation, not the deity Śiva. Identification of Aṣṭāpada with a specific physical peak (often Mount Kailāsa) is traditional and debated; the verse itself only names the tīrtha. Spelling follows the source edition verbatim.
